attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a two week history of worsening breathlessness and facial swelling. She has a medical history of metastatic non-small cell lung cancer, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hypertension. Her regular medications include salbutamol inhaler as required, Symbicort inhaler, metformin, and amlodipine.

On examination, she appears distressed with audible stridor and a swollen face. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 145/90 mmHg, respiratory rate 28/min, SpO2 88% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. Examination of the chest reveals mild expiratory wheeze bilaterally. She has some swelling of her upper limbs.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with signs and symptoms suggestive of superior vena cava obstruction (SVCO), a potentially life-threatening complication of her metastatic non-small cell lung cancer. The acute onset of breathlessness, along with facial swelling and stridor, are classic features of SVCO.

Given the urgency of the situation and the clear clinical picture, the most appropriate next step in management is to administer intravenous dexamethasone. The evidence is tentative regarding the benefit of parenteral glucocorticoids in this indication, however in cases of potential airway compromise it is common clinical practice.

Corticosteroids, such as dexamethasone, help reduce oedema around the obstruction, potentially improving symptoms rapidly. This can buy time for definitive treatment, which may include radiotherapy, chemotherapy or endovascular stenting depending on the underlying malignancy and its responsiveness to treatment.

Nebulised salbutamol might provide some relief for the patient's COPD symptoms, but it would not address the underlying cause of the acute deterioration, which is the SVCO. While bronchodilators may have a role in the overall management, they are not the priority in this emergency situation.

An urgent CT pulmonary angiogram is typically used to diagnose pulmonary embolism. While PE can cause acute breathlessness, it would not explain the facial swelling and stridor. Moreover, in a patient with a clear clinical picture of SVCO, immediate treatment should not be delayed for imaging studies.

Subcutaneous morphine might be considered for symptom relief in palliative care, but it is not the most appropriate first-line treatment for SVCO. The priority is to address the underlying cause of the symptoms rather than just providing symptomatic relief.

Urgent bronchoscopy could be considered to evaluate the extent of airway involvement or to obtain tissue samples. However, it is not the most appropriate next step in this emergency situation. The priority is to stabilise the patient and reduce the oedema associated with SVCO.

After initiating corticosteroid therapy, the next steps would include oxygen therapy if needed, consideration of anticoagulation to prevent thrombosis, and planning for definitive treatment of the underlying malignancy. This may involve oncology and radiation oncology consultations to discuss options such as radiotherapy or systemic therapy.
learningPoints: Superior vena cava obstruction (SVCO) is an oncological emergency that requires prompt recognition and management.
Corticosteroids are the initial treatment of choice for SVCO to reduce oedema and improve symptoms.
Radiotherapy, chemotherapy or endovascular stenting may be considered as definitive treatment for SVCO, depending on the underlying malignancy.
references: Hardy J, Haywood A, Rickett K, Sallnow L, Good P. Practice review: Evidence-based quality use of corticosteroids in the palliative care of patients with advanced cancer. Palliative Medicine. 2021 Mar;35(3):461-72. Available online at ; https://www.sfh.ie/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/Steroids-in-Pall-Care-Article.pdf

Azizi AH, Shafi I, Shah N, Rosenfield K, Schainfeld R, Sista A, Bashir R. Superior vena cava syndrome. Cardiovascular Interventions. 2020 Dec 28;13(24):2896-910. Available online at ; https://www.jacc.org/doi/full/10.1016/j.jcin.2020.08.038
Shah RP, Bolaji O, Duhan S, et al. Superior Vena Cava Syndrome: An Umbrella Review. Cureus. 2023;15(7):e42227. Published 2023 Jul 20. doi:10.7759/cureus.42227
